narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the courage it takes to put yourself out there with your upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend full-time. The admissions model we use predicted a 64% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above the threshold we set for approving applications, leading to the regrettable rejection of yours. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which falls well below the typical student's rank around the middle of their class and strongly pushed the model toward expecting a bar failure. Your LSAT score of 25, significantly lower than the average of about 37, was the second biggest factor pulling in the same concerning direction. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, though below the usual 3.21, offered some counterbalance by nudging the prediction slightly toward passing success. Your family income level and full-time enrollment plans also helped a bit, as they align with patterns seen in students who tend to pass, but they weren't strong enough to overcome the top concerns. Compared to typical applicants, your early Year 1 decile of 2 was only a minor influence and didn't stand out as unusually low. Overall, your profile stood apart from stronger candidates because those two academic rankings and your LSAT score carried the most weight in signaling higher risk to the model. While this outcome stings, especially given the unique challenges many Black women face in competitive fields like law, it's not the end of your path. We encourage you to retake the LSAT to boost that score, focus on improving your class rankings through targeted study habits, and perhaps seek mentorship or prep courses tailored to bar success. Reapplying after addressing these key areas could shift the model's view in your favor—many students turn things around and thrive. You're capable of this, and we're rooting for your next steps. Keep pushing forward.
